topic: Someone finds out they have a half-sibling they never knew about
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, the paper feeling offensively formal in my hand. Inside, a name, a date, and a photograph that made my stomach clench with a cold, tight knot. A half-sibling. A secret I never knew existed, and now it was thrust into my life like a rotten piece of fruit. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp in the quiet apartment. I paced the floor, my jaw tight, every muscle in my body coiled with a sudden, intense need to dismantle whatever this revelation was. I looked at the framed pictures on the shelf—my carefully curated memories—and felt a burning resentment rise up, hot and corrosive. I needed to erase this intrusion, to make this phantom connection vanish before it could poison everything I’d built.

[story 2]

"Of course," I muttered, the word tasting like ash. I stared at the documents spread across the kitchen table, the fluorescent light catching the sheen of my mounting irritation. Another person, another complication. It was as if the universe had decided to hand me an unwelcome burden. I slammed my fist onto the counter, the ceramic rattling precariously. I didn't want this. I didn't want the sudden, unwelcome obligation that this revelation carried. A bitter, sour feeling settled deep in my chest, a refusal to accept the intrusion. I began methodically gathering the papers, stacking them with deliberate, aggressive precision, ensuring every movement was sharp and dismissive, as if crushing the evidence would somehow diminish its existence.

[story 3]

I read the name three times, just to confirm the sheer absurdity of it all. A forgotten branch on a tree I never knew was growing. A wave of pure, unadulterated annoyance washed over me, making my skin prickle with agitation. I stood by the window, watching the city lights blur, feeling utterly disconnected from the sudden, jarring reality unfolding inside my head. It felt like an injustice. Why me? Why now? I turned away from the window, my shoulders stiffening, and began to pace the length of the living room. Each step was heavy, fueled by a simmering, cold fury. I wanted to lash out, to make a scene, to demonstrate how utterly unwelcome this unexpected piece of my lineage truly was.
